title: Surgery

prerequisites.text: Successful completion of all co urses within the preceding academic year.

outline: Objective: The General Surgery programme comprises an 8-week 5th-year course and a 4-week 6th- year course which aim to equip generalist health professionals w ith the ability to provide safe emergency and primary-leve l surgical care. The objective of the 5th year course is to develop a systematised approach to common sy mptoms and pathologies in surgery, and to refine students’ clinical assessment, diagnostic, management, and procedu ral ability. While it is an opportunity to consolidate students’ understandin g of anatomy, physiology, and pa thophysiology, the focus is on becoming competent in generating a differential diag nosis, and to be able to discuss the basic and specialised investigations to exclude and confirm potential causes of a patient’s presentation and guide treatment decisions. This creates the foundation for the 6th year c ourse, where the em phasis is on applying practical diagnostic, management, and procedural skills as student interns. By the end of the course, students will be able to: (a) Describe normal anatomy and physiology, and explain pathophysiology of common surgic al conditions, (b) Identify and categorise common surgical presentations and their underlying causes, using a systematised appr oach, (c) Construct appropriate differential diagnoses for common surgical presentations, (d) Discuss the indications for and interpret basic and specialised investigations to support clinical decision-making, (e) Explain the principles of emergency and primary-level manag ement for common surgical conditi ons, and outline appropriate referral pathways, (f) Demonstrate correct technique in basic surgical procedures under supervision, (g) Apply clinical reasoning to authentic tasks by pr esenting patients and completing clinical documentation, (h) Collaborate effectively as part of a healthcare te am to: Develop and deliver peer teaching presentations, (i) manage a virtual patient; Contribute to a group assignment (j) Reflect on and evaluate their knowledge, skills, and attitudes in preparation for lifelong self-directed learning. Course outline: 242 DEPARTMENTS IN THE FACULTY The 5th-year General Surgery course emphasizes a problem-based and experiential learning approach, integrating theory with clinical exposure at Groote Schuur Hospita l through supported self-directed learning and formal teaching. Key components includ e: (a) Introduction to Surgical Practice: Interdisciplinary week of lectures and workshops preparing students for self-directed clinical learning, (b) Clinical exposure: three 2-week rotations in various surgical sub-speciality units provides the basis for experiential learning (breas t and endocrine, hepatobiliary and p ancreas, upper gastro-intestinal, colorectal, vascular, and acute care surgery), (c) Surgical Textbook: A local ly relevant resource outlining core knowledge, with add itional ‘extended knowl edge’ for advanced l earners, (d) Online Lectures (Amathuba): Self-paced content to complement textbook and clinical exposure, (f) Whole- group tutorials: Daily, cased-based sessions at GS H, OMB, J-floor Seminar room, (g) Small-group tutorials: Case-based discussions within clinical rotations, (h) Formative tutorials: Weekly facilitated group work sessions and formative assignments, (i) Basic surgical skills workshops: Gloving and gowning, knot-tying, suturing, and biopsy.
lecture_times: See module block schedule and official class timetable.
dp_requirements: All scheduled activities are considered compulsory. DP for the General Surgery course is granted for: (a) Full attendance at Introduction to Surgical Practice: Interdisciplinary Week. Students will sign the register for each session. DP withheld if the student misses any session without CMC, (b) Attendance at scheduled clinical activities. Clinical supe rvisor will sign the students’ rotation logbook daily. DP withheld if the student misses more than 4 signatures in total (8 weeks) or more than 2 signatures in a 2-week subspecialty rotation without CMC, (c) Attendance at whole group tutorials. Students will sign the r egister for the daily in-person tuto rial. DP withheld if the student misses more than 80% of the sessions without CMC, (d) Completion of Clinical Exposure Logbook. Students must clerk and log a minimum of 10 patients. At least three cases should follow the patient’s entire journey from pre-op to post-op (Comprehensiv e Portfolio), (e) Attendance at all formative tutorials and completion of group work assignments, (f) Completion of in-course assessment. Students will be marked as competent/ needs remediation for presenting a patient and performing basic surgical skills. Remediation will take place during the block and must be passed to obtain DP, (g) Completion of end-of-block assessments. See breakdown under ‘Assessments ’. Absence exceeding one week (including concession to miss clas s) will require the student to at tend supplementary clinical time. Absence exceeding two weeks (including concession to miss class) will require the student to repeat the course. Should it become apparent that a student has signed a register/ obtained a signature but not attended the session, the case is reported to the Academic Misconduct Committee
assessment: The General Surgery mark comprises three assessments. Students must obtain 50% for each component (sub-minimum) and a weighted overall average of 50% to pass. Students obtaining an average of between 48% and 50% will be recommended to complete a supplementary assessment. Students obtaining less than 48% will be required to repeat the surgical block. (a) In-Course Assessment – weighted 10%. Comp rising group assignments and a ssessment of basic surgical procedures. (b) Integrated Clinical Assessment (ICA) – weighted 30% On the last Thursday of the 8- week rotation, students will have a 1.5 hour written examinati on based on common conditions and basic competencies for Gene ral Surgery. The ICA comprises 50 ma rks and it tests in terpretation as well as practical appli cation of knowledge. (c ) Multiple-Choice Questio n (MCQ) Examination – weighted 60% On the last Friday of the 8-week rotation, students will have a 2.5-hour computer-based Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) e xamination which will test path ophysiology, diagnostic, and management knowledge develo ped during the surgical block. It comprises 100 single-best answer questions and tests core theoretical knowledge and clinical reasoning ability. Students may go back to previous questions and there is no negative marking. S hould a student obtain an overall pass mark but fail one or more sub-components, then they will be recommended to attend supplementary clinical time and complete a supplementary assessment. 1% is awarded as extra credit to students completing the end-of-block evaluation form.
